entry.title: Add Mass Excel Data Updates in any Standard SAP Fiori App Using the UI5 Spreadsheet Importer
entry.content:
Excel remains one of the most critical and widely used tools in the enterprise world, thanks to its versatility and flexibility. Therefore, whenever building new solutions, it is essential to integrate spreadsheet files into digital processes, especially if Excel cannot be fully eliminated.

To simplify this process, I have developed an open-source component that enables you to create entries from spreadsheet files, called UI5 Spreadsheet Importer, and more recently, also allows you to update multiple entries in bulk. The big advantage of this component is that it can be integrated into standard SAP applications. I have previously demonstrated how to do this with the “Manage Banks” application in an earlier blog post (How to add Excel Upload to any Standard SAP Fiori App (tutorial with App "Manage Banks").

In the meantime, many updates have been introduced both to the Spreadsheet Importer component and to how adaptation projects are handled with the Fiori tools. Currently, the UI5 Spreadsheet Importer supports the mass update feature only with OData V4. Therefore, in this blog post, we will use the new “Manage Banks” app, which uses an OData V4 service. Please note that creating new records works with OData V2 and the spreadsheet importer as well.

Deploy Spreadsheet Importer

To use the component, it must be deployed to the ABAP stack. This process has also improved since the last blog post and is documented here.

You can clone this GitHub repository and follow the instructions in the README.md. This repository contains a UI5 application that allows you to import all versions of the Spreadsheet Importer into your system. After deployment, you can reuse the Spreadsheet Importer component in any UI5 apps and adaptation projects.

Create Adaptation Project

The complete example described here can be found in a my GitHub repository.

The creation of adaptation projects has also been improved in the last two years. You can find an excellent tutorial here, and the official documentation is available here .

Here is how it can look like:

Add the Component

Adaptation projects can now also be created in VS Code. The following steps are shown in VS Code, but they do not differ much from SAP Business Application Studio. The general procedure is similar to the one in my previous blog post, although the UI might look a little different now.

First, right-click on the “webapp” folder and choose Add SAPUI5 Component Usages.

In the dialog, provide a Usage ID, for example "customer.upload", and set the component name to the version deployed on your system, for instance "cc.spreadsheetimporter.v1_7_3".

This process will create a file similar to id_1739737700495_addComponentUsages.change.

Adding Custom XML and Custom Controller

To add a button in the toolbar and an extension to trigger the Spreadsheet Importer with JavaScript, you can open the Adaptation Editor by right-clicking on “webapp” and selecting “Open Adaptation Editor”, or by running npm run start-editor from the terminal (as defined in your package.json).

Add Button to Action Toolbar

Hover over the table until it is highlighted with a blue frame, then use the editor’s button to add a new fragment to the Action Toolbar.

In the dialog, choose the “actions” aggregation with the fragment name "excelupload". This creates a new XML file named "excelupload.fragment.xml" under the “fragments” folder, which you can now edit as needed.

 This will create a change file and the XML Fragment.

 

Add Controller Extension

To open the Spreadsheet Importer, you need a Controller Extension. Again, hover over the table and click the button “Extend with Controller”. 

Provide a name for the extension file, for example "excelupload". A new file named "excelupload.js" is generated under the “coding” folder, containing boilerplate code for extending the List Report with JavaScript.

 Make sure to always save the changes. This is highlighted in the Adaption Editor:

This will create a change file and the javascript file.

Add JavaScript Method to Open Spreadsheet Importer

Next, add a method in the controller that will be invoked by our new button. This method calls the Spreadsheet Importer component using the usage ID "customer.upload" we defined earlier.

The Configuration Options are documented here, and these options allow you to tailor the component to your requirements.

In our example, we want to give users the ability to update data in mass. Therefore, we set the property action to "UPDATE". The parameter tableId indicates which table to use for updates. Usually, the best approach is to download your existing data first, so that key fields for identification are automatically included in the spreadsheet. Hence, we enable a download button by setting showDownloadButton to true.

We also configure deepDownloadConfig properties:

  • deepLevel: 0, because we only need the root level entity.
  • addKeysToExport: true, so that the spreadsheet will contain key fields for identification.
  • setDraftStatus: true, to handle drafts properly if the OData V4 service uses draft features.
  • showOptions: false, to skip any additional dialogs and immediately download the spreadsheet.

The final method looks like this:

 

async onUpload(event) {
    const spreadsheetUploadComponent = await this.base.getAppComponent().createComponent({
        usage: "customer.upload",
        async: true,
        componentData: {
            context: this,
            action: "UPDATE",
            tableId: "bf.bk.bank.manage::BankList--fe::table:‌‌:LineItem-innerTable",
            showDownloadButton: true,
            deepDownloadConfig: {
                deepLevel: 0,
                addKeysToExport: true,
                setDraftStatus: true,
                showOptions: false
            }
        }
    });

    spreadsheetUploadComponent.openSpreadsheetUploadDialog();
}

 

Add Button to Call Method

Finally, we add a button in the XML fragment to call our onUpload method. An example XML fragment could look like this:

 

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core='sap.ui.core' xmlns='sap.m'>
    <Button 
        id="excelUpload" 
        text="Excel Upload"
        press=".extension.customer.app.variant1.excelupload.onUpload"
    />
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

Note that in the press event, the namespace of your extension must be included. For example, if your extension namespace is "customer.app.variant1" and the controller extension is named "excelupload", the method is "onUpload".

The Button now appears in the Toolbar:

 

Preview

You can now open the preview from the context menu (right-click on webapp and select “Preview Application”) or start it from the terminal with npm start. This will launch the application, and when you click on your new “Excel Upload” button, the Spreadsheet Importer dialog will appear.


From there, you can click “Download Data as Spreadsheet” to edit and then re-upload your data.

 In this example, you might add a new value (like a BIC code) for all banks in the spreadsheet and upload it.

The “Show Preview” button lets you see the data before finalizing the upload.

Clicking the “Upload” button then updates the data in the background.

Further Changes

A quick note of caution: Not every app can be adapted with the Adaptation Project, and each standard app may behave differently. Also, for the update feature, the underlying standard app must use an OData V4 service. Creating new records, however, is also possible with OData V2. Simply change the action to "CREATE" or add a separate button for creation.

Additionally, there are many other configuration options and events you can leverage to customize the component to your specific needs.

Deployment

For deployment of the adaptation project, you can follow this documentation .

Further Links

  • Landing Page UI5 Spreadsheet Importer
  • Live Demo Spreadsheet Importer and Mass Updates
  • Central Deployment of the Spreadsheet Importer
  • GitHub Repo for Packed Deployment to ABAP
  • Configuration Options for Spreadsheet Importer
  • SAPUI5 Adaptation Tutorial
  • Official Documentation for Adaptation Projects
  • Deployment Instructions for Adaptation Projects

best regards, Peter

entry.title: Re: Custom field appearing twice
entry.content:
Hello,

You can try the following:

1. **Custom Field Visibility and Enablement**:
- When you create custom fields, ensure that you have enabled their usage for all relevant user interfaces and APIs. It seems like there's a disconnect between the SAP GUI and the UI where the fields are displayed.

2. **SAP GUI Enablement**:
- Enabling the SAP GUI for the custom field could be causing the duplication on the UI if the field was initially intended for a different interface. Ensure the field is only enabled in the necessary contexts.

3. **Field Visibility in APIs**:
- When you noticed the field didn’t appear in the UI after creation via API, but it was in the database, it indicates that the field might not have been enabled correctly across all necessary UIs. Ensure the field is enabled for API usage in the "APIs" tab and check if it aligns with how custom fields should be used with API operations.

4. **Check Custom Field Definition**:
- It might be useful to revisit the Custom Fields app to ensure you haven’t configured the same custom field under two different contexts or mistakenly created two similar fields.

Best regards,

Dequan Xu

entry.title: Re: How to attach FE OP Controller Extension function to...
entry.content:
I would suggest you attach to the List Binding Event:
https://ui5.sap.com/#/api/sap.ui.model.odata.v4.ODataListBinding%23events/dataReceived 

onAfterRendering: function () {
  var oModel = this.base.getExtensionAPI().getModel();
  var oView = this.getView();
  var oTable = oView.byId("yourTableId"); // Replace with actual table ID
  
  if (oTable) {
    var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items"); // Adapt this if you're using a different aggregation
    if (oBinding) {
      oBinding.attachEvent("dataReceived", function (oEvent) {
        console.log("Data received:", oEvent);
      });
    }
  }
}

entry.title: SAP SIGNAVIO PROCESS INTELLIGENCE: Unkown IP adress appearing during SAP RFC connection
entry.content:
We are trying to set up a connection between a SAP ECC 7.5 SP31 system and SAP Signavio Process Intelligence Process Intelligence using the SAP RFC connection option ( no On premise connector). We are getting the following error
"Checking credentials: rejected. Reason: Initialization of destination ... failed. ".

Our suspicion is that this may be related to some blocked IP adresses. We saw that the following IP adress is sending packets "46.105.70.90" on ports ports 20200 and 20500. I was unable to find this IP adress in any Signavio documentation, or any SAP documentation for that matter.

I wanted to ask if anyone has encountered this problem and this IP adress before and whether anyone knows if this is an SAP related IP adress?
I would also be very happy about any solution to this issue

entry.title: Re: How to Configure "standard text key" for task list &...
entry.content:
If you are unable to find the "Define Standard Text Key" option in SPRO,
follow these steps to configure and use the Standard Text Key correctly in SAP PM:

Step 1: Check Configuration for Standard Text Key
1.Go to SPRO (SAP Customizing Implementation Guide)
- Navigate to:
Plant Maintenance and Customer Service → Maintenance Plans, Work Centers, Task Lists → Task Lists → General Data
- Look for an option related toStandard Text Key.
- If you cannot find it, check if the relevant configuration is available under:
Plant Maintenance and Customer Service → Maintenance and Service Processing → Basic Settings → Define Standard Text Key

2. If the option is still not visible, ensure that you have the necessary authorization to make changes in SPRO.

Step 2: Assign Standard Text Key to Task List
1. UseTransaction IA01 (Create Equipment Task List).
2. In theOperation Details, navigate to theExternal Processing tab.
3. Enter theStandard Text Key in the relevant field.

Step 3: Use Standard Text Key in Maintenance Order
1. UseTransaction IW31 (Create Maintenance Order).
2. When selecting aTask List, the Standard Text Key should automatically be copied into the order operation.

Step 4: Maintain Standard Texts in SO10
1. UseTransaction SO10 to create predefined texts.
2. Link the Standard Texts to the Standard Text Key.

Alternative Solution (If Standard Text Key Is Not Available)
If the "Define Standard Text Key" option is not available in SPRO, you can achieve similar functionality using:
-Work Instructions in Task Lists
-Long Text in Operations (IA01, IA02, IA03)
-Catalog Profiles (Custom Codes for Standard Texts)

entry.title: Re: Datasphere replication flow Initial load in progress...
entry.content:
We identified the root cause why data is not loading for I_XXXXXXX . This is due to Datasphere Model editor bug which introduced wrong datatype definition for columns IntcoExtActlTransfOfCtrlDteTme, IntcoIntActlTransfOfCtrlDteTme, IntcoExtPlndTransfOfCtrlDteTme, and IntcoExtActlTransfOfCtrlDteTme timestamp columns.

This bug has been fixed in Datasphere version 2025.3.66.

Option 1: Go to Data Builder Replication Flow editor, delete I_XXXXXXX object., add the object. Then deploy. If you do not want to delete the replication object, then follow these manual steps 2-9

2. Go to Data Builder editor

3. Use Edit -> “Export to CSN/JSON File” from the replication flow editor and export the CSN definition of the RF

4. Open the exported file, search for $DYNAMIC.decimal_undefined_undefined under the section “replicationTasks” and replace it with com.sap.core.timestamp. Repeat this for all occurrences of $DYNAMIC.decimal_undefined_undefined in the file and replace with com.sap.core.timestamp

5. After the above changes are made, save the file.

6. Navigate to Data Builder landing page and import the above file using the option Import Objects from CSN/JSON File

7. Deploy the replication flow from the landing page (Please do not deploy the replication flow from the editor)

Please note that when you modify the replication flow definition externally, you should only change the recommended fields as any wrong changes can break the replication flow.

entry.title: Compliance Performance Visibility: Exploring the New Compliance Overview Dashboard
entry.content:
Managing environmental compliance can often feel overwhelming—tracking overdue tasks, notices of violation, deviations, and incidents all at once is no small task. 

But what if you could have ongoing visibility into these aspects? Enter the new Compliance Overview dashboard in SAP Analytics Cloud—a game-changer for your EHS team looking to get better transparency into compliance performance and focus on what matters most. 

 

What’s on the Dashboard?

Key Metrics at a Glance 

  • Tasks: A breakdown of the compliance tasks by priority and status. 
  • Deviations: Number of recorded compliance deviations. 
  • Notices of Violation: Count of issued notices, paired with total penalty amounts. 
  • Environmental Incidents: A snapshot of incidents categorized by type (e.g. air, water, etc.). 
  • Overdue Tasks: Total count of compliance tasks that have surpassed their due dates. 

By adjusting the Plant and Priority filters at the top, you can tailor all these metrics to focus on specific locations or priorities, giving you targeted insights for precise decision-making. 

 

Zooming in: Interactive Graphs 

Tasks Overview 

Compliance tasks are reactive or proactive action steps, which help you minimize your environmental impact or prevent environmental incidents and deviations. 

The Tasks Overview graph displays these tasks filtered by priority (e.g. Very High, Medium, Low) and status (e.g. Scheduled, Overdue, Completed), providing a clear view of task progress year-to-date (YTD). While this is the default setup, the dashboard’s flexibility allows you to adjust the graph types and display information based on your preference. 

 

Deviations  

A deviation refers to a departure from approved procedures, standards, permits or regulations, i.e. it’s used to handle cases of non-conformance to specific environmental requirements. 

The Deviations graph provides a YTD breakdown similar to the Tasks Overview, categorizing the deviations by origin (e.g. Permit, Policy, etc.) and type (Reportable or Nonreportable) offering customizable visualizations that help track patterns and adjust focus areas. With this flexibility, you can tailor the data display to identify trends and prioritize corrective actions. 

 

Notices of Violation 

A notice of violation is a formal warning from regulatory authorities indicating a breach of an environmental regulation. 

In this graph, you’ll see both the total penalty amounts and the number of violations recorded. Using the dropdown field, you can adjust what’s displayed on the graph, offering a dynamic view of the data that’s most relevant to your compliance goals. 

 

Environmental Incidents 

An environmental incident is any unplanned event that harms the environment, such as hazardous spills, pollutant emissions, or waste discharges affecting air, water, soil, etc. 

The Environmental Incidents section categorizes these events by release type, such as air, ground, water, or your custom release. You can use filters to view specific types or display all categories for a broader view. This flexibility helps pinpoint areas requiring immediate attention or analyze trends across multiple types of incidents. 

 

Tracking Trends 

The Compliance Overview dashboard also provides insights to trend analysis: 

 

Incidents Over Time 

This trend graph displays the number of incidents by type (e.g., deviations, violations, etc.) over time, allowing you to identify recurring patterns, pinpoint high-risk periods, and address them proactively. 

 

Overdue Tasks Trend 

This graph shows the monthly trend of overdue tasks for the past six months of the current calendar year. It helps you spot bottlenecks and assess your team's capacity to handle compliance tasks within deadlines. 

 

 

 

Why All This Matters 

The Compliance Overview dashboard isn’t just a collection of numbers—it’s a tool designed to empower environmental managers: 

  • Identify Risks: By visualizing overdue tasks, deviations, and incidents, you can quickly pinpoint areas of concern and take corrective action. 
  • Save Time: With all critical metrics in one place, you spend less time hunting for data and more time solving problems. 
  • Always Up-To-Date: The dashboard pulls live data directly from EHS applications, ensuring the insights you’re working with are accurate. 
  • Adaptable to Your Needs: The graphs and filters are fully customizable, allowing you to tailor the dashboard to fit your team’s unique priorities and workflows. 

With the Compliance Overview dashboard, you gain the clarity and control needed to turn complex data into actionable insights. Whether you’re tracking trends, managing tasks, or assessing deviations, this tool ensures you’re always one step ahead.

What can we do make sure we always use application/json when calling this web hook?

Regards

Mattias

entry.title: ABAP Data Integration for SAP Business Data Cloud: configuration, performance, sizing
entry.content:
ABAP Data Integration for SAP Business Data Cloud introduces a new technology called ABAP Push Replication Service (APRS). APRS pushes the data of CDS views, which are part of Data Products, into HANA Data Lake Files (HDLFS).

In this blog I want to give you an overview of the APRS and introduce you to important performance settings.

Connection Configuration

The rather complex task of connections configuration between SAP BDC, APRS and HDLFS is described in the SAP Note 3539174 - Integration of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Business Data Cloud. We assume it has been done and everything is working.

 

Introduction to APRS

The operation of APRS is based on several background tasks (the way how jobs are working you can see on  Figure 2):

- (ACD) Job and Access Plan Calculation (ACP) Job: processes for calculating the optimal transfer of an initial snapshot of the CDS View. Those processes capture the initial snapshot of object and start data transfer upon completing the calculation of portion. The process is especially important for transferring large objects, as it determines the parameters for parallelizing data transfer.

On Figure 1 you can find an example how CDS view is divided into ACD partitions, access plans and portions. ACD partitions are necessary to parallelize the execution of pre-calculation of CDS views where physical table partitioning exists.

A single access plan contains a 10 Gb of data and is further divided into 100 portions, 100 Mb each. Access Plan Pre-Calculation Job creates access plans. Access plan is restricted by the boundaries, which are conditions on the key fields of a CDS view. Portion has no boundaries, but simple a block of data 100 Mn size.

Parallelization of ACD jobs for a CDS view is only  possible when main table contain DB partitions. Parallelization of ACP jobs depend on the number of access plans for the CDS view.

Figure 1: Access Plan calculation concept

- Observer Job: monitors changes in logging tables, put new request in the queue for data transfer, starts the transfer jobs if they are not already running.

- Transfer Job: the process responsible for data transfer. It handles both the initial data load and the transfer of delta.

- Health Check Job: performs various functions related to monitoring the service's health (like starting missing observer jobs, verifying the status of portions that have been in process for a long time or verifying the daemon status), and as part of it health check calculates the optimal number of required transfer jobs. By default, it runs every 10 minutes.

Since the APRS relies on background processes, it is essential to ensure the correct configuration of the technical job framework before using the service. The list of required background jobs and links to configuration instructions can be found in SAP Note 3500131 - ABAP Data Integration for Business Data Cloud.

Figure 2: Job diagram

Access plan Calculation job and Transfer job are running during initial load, Observer job and Transfer job are running during delta replication. Health Check job is always running when at least one subscription exists.

APRS contains a number of parameters for fine-tuning system performance (see Figure 3). By default, all parameters are set , obtained from testing of standard use cases. Some parameters require adjustments if it is necessary to allocate more resources to APRS than were defined by the default settings. The settings that affect performance can be modified using the DHADM tool and are shown in Figure 3.

Figure 3: Job Settings

Note: by default parameter names are displayed in a readable format. You can switch to the technical parameter names through the menu: Utilities -> Display Parameter Name

 

Configuring Initial Load

Access Plan Calculation

With the implemented SAP TCI Notes ABAP Integration - released October 2024 (see SAP Note 3500131 - ABAP Data Integration for Business Data Cloud) is used to orchestrate ACD and ACP jobs.

Note: The parameter "/1DH/ACS*" / "Maximum Number of Jobs for ABAP Daemon" (ACP_MAX_JOBS) defines the maximum number of background jobs that can be allocated for ACP processes. The default value (-1) means that the number of background jobs is unlimited, but it cannot exceed 80% of all configured background processes in the system.

The access plan pre-calculation job determines the optimal conditions for the following parallelization of access plan calculation. The parameters are calculated according to the amount of data required for transfer. The parameters are described in SAP Note 3559452 - ABAP Data Integration - Automatic parallelization for access plan calculation jobs.

Figure 4: Jobs for access plan calculation

The DHADM transaction allows you to change the default values for the object priority and the maximum number of background processes for each object individually.

Transfer Jobs

Transfer Job performs the transferring data to the target system. The same Job can transfer data of initial load and delta load. All parameters for the Transfer job are relevant for both initial loading and delta replication. Here we will consider all the parameters affecting the transfer job.

The initial data transfer to HDLFS consists of several phases: reading of data from intermediate tables, converting data to CSV format and writing data to the data lake. Therefore, the speed of data transfer in HDLFS depends on numerous APRS parameters and the performance of the system itself.

During the initial load, the unit of data transfer is a portion created during the ACP calculation. This means that multiple portions can be transferred through several parallel processes. See Figure 4 where you can find tab Portions. On this tab you find list of all calculated portions that are ready for transfer process. Column “Portion Status” tells what is actual transfer status for portion: Open – transfer not yet started, In Process, Finished or Failed – if any error occurs during transfer. The "Transfer End Time" column is filled in if the transfer of this portion is completed without errors (see Figure 5).

Figure 5: List of Portions for Data Transfer

By default, the number of Transfer Jobs is set to 1. If the number of objects is very large or the amount of data in one or several objects is large (for example, the CDS View I_GLAccountLineItemRawData is traditionally the largest object and often contains billions of records), it highly recommended increase the number of parallel jobs (parameter TRANSFER_MAX_JOBS). The parameters "/1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA" / "Minimum Number of Data Transfer Jobs" (TRANSFER_MIN_JOBS) and "Maximum Number of Data Transfer Jobs" (TRANSFER_MAX_JOBS) define the lower and upper limits for the number of concurrently running background processes. The Health Check calculates the required number of processes and schedules new ones in the configured range (TRANSFER_MIN_JOBS – TRANSFER_MAX_JOBS) if required with each run. The number of running processes can be seen in DHADM in the Transfer Overview section under the Transfer Jobs tab (see Figure 6).

Figure 6: List of active Transfer Jobs

The parameter "/1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA" / "Minimum Runtime for Data Transfer Job" (TRANSFER_MIN_RUNTIME_SEC) is necessary to set the minimum "lifetime" of the background process. This is necessary in cases where there are a large number of changes in the system with a short time between changes. If the job does not find data to work with, it immediately stops, and starting a new job takes a certain amount of time. And additionally, to avoid generating a large number of jobs with a short lifetime, this parameter can be used to set the time the process will wait for new data to be available for transferring. To avoid degrading system performance, it is not recommended to set the parameter below the default value.

 

Configurations for Delta Replication

Observer Jobs

If the CDS View is subscribed for delta replication, a set of Master and Subscriber Logging tables is created for each table described in the corresponding annotations to the CDS View. Any changes made to the data in the table are recorded in the corresponding Master Logging table. The Observer Job monitors the contents of the Master Logging tables and copies these records to the Subscriber Logging tables with simultaneous transformations necessary to increase data transfer speed. At the same time, a new entry is created in the queue for the Transfer Job. By default, only one Observer Job is running in the system, which is sufficient in most cases for copying changes in the system. If there are significant changes in the system, with several million records per second in different tables, and you observe that the number of records in the "No. of Records in Master Logging Table" column in the Recording Overview section of DHADM is not decreasing, you can increase the number of Observer Jobs by changing the parameters "/1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB" / "Maximum Number of Observer Jobs" (OBSERVER_MAX_JOBS) or "Minimum Number of Observer Jobs" (OBSERVER_MIN_JOBS). After the next run of the Health Check Job, the number of Observer Jobs will change within the established values. If the minimum and maximum amounts are different, the number of background processes will change automatically depending on the system's needs.

By default, the lifetime of a single Observer Job is set to one hour. If necessary, for example, if the number of changes in the system is small, you can change the parameters "/1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB" / "Observer Job Runtime" (OBSERVER_MIN_RUNTIME) to adjust the runtime of the Observer Job and "Observer Job Wait Time" (OBSERVER_PERIOD), which determines the pause between the next run of the Observer Job. Keep in mind that if you change the parameter "Observer Job Wait Time", you will increase latency the processing of changes.

Figure 7: List of active Observer Jobs

Transfer Jobs

As mentioned earlier, the transfer job works both during the initial data load and delta replication. You can find information about the parameters directly affecting the transfer job in the description for initial load phase. Here you will find additional information about the transfer process that is relevant only during delta replication.

During delta load, changes are accumulated in special buffer tables (RDB) and transferred as enough data is collected or after some time interval has passed. By default the transfer process starts if one of two conditions is met: either the size of the changes exceed 10 Mb or the changes accumulate in buffer over 10 minutes.

Note: the Delta Data Transfer Daemon (see Figure 2) is observe the conditions for delta buffer and creates a new queue entry of conditions are fulfil.

During the subscription start, the system automatically calculates two parameters for the buffer table: the maximum buffer size (in number of records) and the size of one packet (also measured in the number of records).

The buffer is regularly replenished by reconstructing the modified data. When the buffer reaches the maximum number of records, it is blocked for writing and waits for the moment when the packets formed from the buffer data will be transferred to the target. The packets are formed by the transfer job itself before the data transfer starts. And package is the smallest unit for the transfer process during delta replication.

Therefore, the buffer size and packet size are important for optimal data transfer speed. Ideally, we need to achieve parameters such that the buffer never overflows, and the packets are of sufficient size for the transfer process to handle them quickly enough.

Note: the complete list of all job settings parameters is described in SAP Note 3223735.

 

Performance / Sizing

Based on the service's experience with large volumes of replicable data, here are some recommendations for fine-tuning the previously mentioned parameters.

Access Plan Calculation

After installing SAP Note 3559452, adjusting parameters to increase ACP performance is not required. It should be mentioned that the speed of the pre-calculation process depends on the presence of partitions for the main table in the CDS View. In this case, even the pre-calculation process is carried out in several parallel processes. Partitioning is especially recommended for the ACDOCA table, which is the basis table for the CDS View I_GLAccountLineItemRawData, as well as for other tables containing huge volumes of data.

Observer Jobs

The Observer Job does not affect the initial load and is only relevant for delta replication. You should only increase the number of jobs if you observe a significant number of records in the Master Logging Tables and this number either does not decrease significantly over a long period of time or even grows. Do not enable the collection of Observer Job statistics unless it is necessary to analyze the process's throughput. Do not leave the statistics collection enabled for more than 1-2 days.

Transfer Jobs

When calculating the required resources for data transfer, keep in mind that all objects have different record lengths. With the same number of records, an object with 2-5 fields will load several times faster than an object where one record consists of 400 fields. But you should also not forget about the field types. The same small table can consist entirely of string fields where each value can contain megabytes of data.

Throughput measurements on large data volumes show an approximate size of 5-6 megabytes per second.

If you have objects like the CDS View I_GLAccountLineItemRawData containing billions of records, it makes sense to configure 40-50 background processes in the system and set the maximum number of processes for transfer jobs to 30-35. The minimum number of processes in this case is recommended to be 10. In this case, APRS has the ability to flexibly adjust the number of transfer jobs, and the data transfer for a single object will be maximally parallelized.

At the end of the initial load, the number of background processes can be significantly reduced. Depending on the volume of the changes in the system, it is recommended to set the number of running background processes to between 1 and 10.

 

Configuration example

Measurements on some test systems show the following metrics for the "large" object I_GLAccountLineItemRawData (consists of 437 fields):
Calculation time for one single standard access plan takes from 300 to 420 sec. One access plan consists of 100 portions. One portion contains ~16000 records.
Transfer time for one portion is from 15 to 23 sec.

We suggest follow formula to estimate number of batch processes necessary to fulfil calculation phase in desirable time frame:

N(max number of ACP jobs) = S(number of records in  CDS View) / T(sec in time frame) * F(number of fields in CDS View) / 350.000

Example: Assuming a view having 437 fields and 3 billion records you want to finish calculation phase in 24 hours. 3.000.000.000 / 86400 (sec in one day) * 437 / 350.000 (average number of processed field in one second) = 45 access plan jobs will be required.

 

CSV generation is a CPU-intensive task. Transfer performance can be roughly estimated at 150,000 - 300,000 cells/sec. This varies greatly depending on the actual field content and data types.

We suggest follow formula to estimate number of batch processes necessary to fulfil data transfer phase in desirable time frame:

N(max number of transfer jobs) = S(number of records in  CDS View) / T(sec in time frame) * F(number of fields in CDS View) / 300.000

Example: Assuming a view having 437 fields and 3 billion records you want to load the data in 24 hours. 3.000.000.000 / 86400 (sec in one day) * 437 / 300.000 (number of processing field in one second) = 50 transfer jobs will be required.

 

Taking in account that transfer process starts as soon as first access plan is calculated and further both phases running parallel, our tests have shown following numbers for CDS view I_GLAccountLineItemRawData containing 3 billions records:

  • pre-calculation and calculation in 10 parallel jobs takes ~1.5 days
  • transfer time in 30 parallel jobs takes ~1.5-2 days

entry.title: Re: Pros & Cons of using Stand-alone SLT vs Embedded SLT
entry.content:
Hi Mainak,

There are limitations of the S/4 HANA embedded version of SLT (see KBA 2580573). This said there are two main differences between embedded and standalone SLT:

  1. When using a database connection to a source or target, SLT on S/4 HANA (S4CORE or DMIS 2020 on S/4 HANA foundation) can only use a database connection to a HANA database. Standalone SLT (DMIS 2011 or DMIS 2018) can replicate from and to third party databases listed in SAP Note 1768805. 
  2. Standalone SLT has a lower NetWeaver version than embedded SLT, so can have problems with unsupported data types (see KBA 2985946). Standalone SLT therefore needs to apply a workaround in such cases.

Regards,
Ian
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Manage your project documentation using SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hello Olga,
thanks for your question. We plan to have documents also available cross projects in future. Currently you can only create document within a project. But there is a common workaround for these reusable elements: we have library elements, which are cross project available. If you assign a document to such a library element, the document is available everywhere where the element is used.

currently we have application library elements, soon we will also have a solution activity library elements. then you can add the document to activity in a diagram/process and then assign the document to the activity. then, the document is available everywhere.

you can find this example in my recorded session from the ALM summit 2024: https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-almsummit/sap-alm-summit-emea-2024-materials.html

this video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5ZYZXzmvB8k (minute 16:00)

In case you have more questions, please let me know.

 

cheers

Sinje

entry.title: How to consume RAP entity from other ABAP System?
entry.content:
Hello community,

we recently developed a RAP API as managed scenario for a custom table on-premise. We were able to provide a REST API within minutes, which is really great.

Now we need to make use of this API from another SAP on-premise system. Lets say, in our ERP system we build an API for a table Z_USERDATA. We can use EML to make use of this API locally in our ERP system. -> Great!
We can use REST to make use of this API from a 3rd-party app -> Great again!!

Now I need to access this API from our on-premise SAP HR system. In past, we would develop a set of  RFC function modules to read and change data. I want to avoid it and I'm curious what's the best way to access the API frmo another SAP system?

Is there something like EML with RFC functionallity? If not, can someone guide me on how to access the API from ABAP system? As said, we're running both SAP systems on-premise.

Thanks Patrick

entry.title: No dedicated error message found (SWF_FLEX_WEBGUI010) in SAP GUI for workflow scenario WS02000471
entry.content:
SAP standard flexible workflow scenarion WS02000471 is activated and used for purchase requisition item approval. In some cases (particularly in non-production environments) an approver can't be identified because the master data used to identify approvers hasn't been defined.

An enhancement implementation for Responsibility Management (RSM_BADI_STATIC_RULE) identifies approvers. When an exception is raised while "ON_CREATION_CALLBACK" is processed, the workflow log shows the exception hierarchy, but the exception is not considered a business exception (IS_BUSINESS_EXCEPTION is not set in SWWLOGHIST).

This seems to be the reason ME53N displays the message "No dedicated error message found" (SWF_FLEX_WEBGUI010) when a user presses the button labeled "error" in the "Approval Details" subscreen.
After discovering the above I updated the table entry (in our development system) and confirmed that the root cause message from the exception raised in our BAdI implementation was displayed in SAP GUI as expected.

Now, the question is whether this is "just" a limitation we have to live with, or if anyone has found a way to get workflow history log entries identified correctly as business exceptions? 

PS: I am aware that I can report this to SAP, so that's not the suggestion I am looking for. If this post doesn't helps me find a solution I may create a support case - but sometimes the response is just that it works as designed when it's not clearly an error.

entry.title: Re: Would i able to do stay certified assessment by purch...
entry.content:
HI @siva_raman2 

SAP Learning Hub is the exclusive solution that provides everything you need to get and stay certified, with access to yearly assessments. These assessments are short, non-proctored quizzes with unlimited attempts, and must be successfully completed before your certification expiry date. Alternatively, if you don’t have a subscription to SAP Learning Hub, you will need to retake the certification exam(s) every year, by purchasing SAP Certification exam, one attempt or SAP Certification exam, six attempts.
For more information on these purchase options, you can visit this SAP Certification FAQ.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Does the CL_BCS_MAIL_MESSAGE API (e-mail via RAP) allow whitelisting of recipients?
entry.content:
The API CL_BCS_MAIL_MESSAGE is not only available on BTP but since 2022 also for S/4HANA. The old e-mail frameworks allowed the whitelisting of recipients in transaction SCOT. By maintaining a list of recipients in transaction SCOT, it was possible to restrict who should receive e-mails e.g. from a DEV or QA system. This is a common procedure to prevent sending e-mails to externals on dev and qa environments.

The whitelisting via SCOT does not apply to the new framework. Is there a similar functionality or a BAdI available?

entry.title: Re: SAP PS - How to add new option as a PR for Network >>...
entry.content:
Hi Tanuj.

These options are standard in the system. They vary depending on the material master data in MM (T-codes MM01 and/or MM02) and the information maintained in the MRP tabs. In the material master, it is determined whether the material is produced in-house, procured externally, managed in individual stock, collective stock, or both, etc.

If you are trying to use a different purchase requisition document type, be aware that, by default, only one document type can be used (T-code OPTT). In other words, it is not possible to use two different document types, such as NB and ZENO. To achieve this, you will need to use the user exit COZF0002.

Regards
Marcio Blos

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Transformation Center - Digital Blueprint
entry.content:
Hi, SAP BTC recently released a "Time Slice" solution pattern to selectively exclude data before a defined fiscal year from the scope. Currently this is supported for FI (Financial Accounting) and CO (Controlling) applications. Transactional data that doesn't come from FI and CO applications is migrated, independently of the time slice. 
You can check https://help.sap.com/docs/btc/application-help/time-slice for more details.

table TBTCO, have a look at fields PERIODIC, PRDMINS, PRDHOURS...

Kind regards

Jan

entry.title: Budget Configuration: Prevent Exceeding Budget at Country Level
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I have a requirement regarding the budget configuration, and I would like to know if anyone has set up something similar.

In a compensation template with a scope covering multiple countries, a manager can have employees from different countries in their form. The current budget rule is that the budget should not be exceeded. However, the rule only triggers if the total budget is exceeded, and the client wants the system to prevent exceeding the budget at the country level.

At the moment, the budget is configured as follows:

  • Mode: Percent of Custom Field
  • Used For: Salary
  • Budget Value: 100
  • Based On: Template

Budget Rule:

  • Used For: Allow exceed - Disallow

Budget Grouping:

  • Group By Field 1: Country
  • Show Total Budget: Yes

Has anyone encountered a similar scenario or configured something like this before? Any suggestions or insights would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: YMCR. SCope - add admin change - configuration item i...
entry.content:
Hello Raghav,

You mention you're using continual cycle with no task.  Are you meaning there is no task list created?  With no task list then the project track wouldn't be known and thus not able to save the configuration item.  See KBA 3064943 for more information.  General change is exception.

Thanks,

Diana

entry.title: Automatic Planning with Technician-Specific Starting Locations and Fixed Work
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

We are setting up automatic planning in SAP FSM, and I would like to ask if anyone has experience or a solution for handling the following scenarios:

  1. Technician’s Starting Location Changes Within the Month

    • For example, a technician starts work from home for the first 20 days of the month but then switches to another address (e.g., a girlfriend’s place) for the remaining days.
    • Can SAP FSM’s automatic planning accommodate such changes dynamically? I was thinking about using UDO but according to the documentation's limitation, it's not possible

  2. Fixed Work Areas on Specific Days of the Week

    • Some technicians have predefined fixed locations on certain weekdays.
    • Example:
      • Monday: Only working in the town Prague
      • Tuesday: Only working in the town Brno
    • How can we set up automatic planning so that jobs are scheduled according to these predefined regional preferences per weekday?

We have a large batch of tickets at the beginning of each month and need to plan them as efficiently as possible for the upcoming month. Any insights, best practices, or configuration recommendations would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you in advance for any input.

Filip

entry.title: Purchase order & Purchase Requisition Release Strategy: for multiple order currency
entry.content:
Hello Folks,

I am working on a project involving two company code enterprise structures. These two company codes have different local currencies (e.g., Company A uses KES, and Company B uses MMK), with USD as the group currency.

I am facing an issue with the currency conversion exchange rate in the Purchase Order currency.

For the release strategy, I have made the following configurations:

  • Configured the release strategy by maintaining the Purchase Order net order value CEKKO(GNETW) [Characteristic currency is USD], Purchase Order Type CEKKO(BSART), and Plant CEKKO(EWERK).

Now, when I create a Purchase Order with a third currency (EUR) for Company A, the system, according to SAP Note FAQ 493900 Q.13, converts the order currency into the company code currency and then from the company code currency into the release strategy currency (EUR > KES > USD).

The exchange rates maintained in OB08 are as follows:

  • Exchange rate type: M
    • 1 EUR = 134.02 KES
    • 1 KES = 0.0077 USD

Release Strategy Configuration:

  • Release Strategy 1: PO net order value ≤ 100.00 USD
  • Release Strategy 2: PO net order value > 100.00 – 150.00 USD

Issue:

I created a Purchase Order in EUR currency with a net order value of 99.00 EUR.

The system should convert this value as follows:

  • 99.00 EUR = 13,266.99 KES
  • 13,266.99 KES = 102.69 USD

Based on the above currency conversion, Release Strategy 2 should be triggered. However, the system is triggering Release Strategy 1 instead. I tested this multiple times with different close values to the two release strategies, but I continue to face inconsistent currency conversion, leading to the incorrect release strategy being triggered.

My question to you all:

Is the system using a different exchange rate type(apart from M) for the conversion between the order currency > local currency > release strategy characteristic currency?

#SAP MM # PO RELEASE STRATEGY # SAP S/4HANA

entry.title: Environment, Health, and Safety in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502
entry.content:
With this blog I will walk you through my selected key highlights for Environment, Helth and Saftey in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Pubic Edition 2502:

  • New Application for Managing the Investigation Process,
  • Integration with Learning Management Systems 
  • Electronic Submission of Recordable Injury or Illness Cases to OSHA 
  • Automatic Generation of Emissions Declarations
  • Create and Manage Requests for Chemical Approval

In case you do not need all the information below and just briefly want to get an overview of the release highlights, please watch this video with the top two key innovations:

Video #1: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 for Environment, Health, and Safety

In case your EHS team wants to get better transparency into compliance performance using the new Compliance Overview dashboard in SAP Analytics Cloud, please kindly explore the blog post Compliance Performance Visibility: Exploring the New Compliance Overview Dashboard.

New Application for Managing the Investigation Process

Business Background

Investigations help in identifying the underlying causes of accidents or incidents, whether they are related to human factors, equipment failure, procedural errors, or environmental conditions. This thorough analysis is essential to develop effective corrective and preventive actions.

In the same way, properly managing investigations ensures compliance with legal requirements and regulatory standards. It demonstrates an organization's commitment to investigating incidents thoroughly and taking necessary actions to prevent similar incidents in the future.

Key Feature

In the previous release, this feature was available as an optional feature. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502, it is generally available and doesn’t require any activation.

It is enabling the investigation leaders to manage the investigation process and document the investigation result in a separate application and empowering expert users to manage the investigation result without access to the incident. The user need only read access to the incidents.

It is allowing investigation team to Plan the investigation process, Collect evidence, Analyze root causes and make recommendations to prevent the future incidents. For more information, see Manage Investigations and following Demo.

 


Video #2: DEMO of Manage Investigations App

Integration with Learning Management Systems

Business Background

Previously, when an incident investigation warranted a learning assignment, the process involved several manual steps within the Learning Management System. First, the Incident Manager had to notify the HR or Line Manager of the relevant Learning Assignee. Then, the Line Manager would assign the learning tasks in the Learning Management System for the Assignee. Finally, any changes to the learning task status had to be manually communicated back to the Incident Manager.

With this new release, you will be able to assign content from an integrated learning management system (LMS) to employees who need to fulfill certain training, qualification, or certification requirements.

Key Feature

In the new Task Definition: Learning Task app (F8398), you can create a task for an employee to complete a learning in an integrated learning management system. You can select a learning from a list of items that are available and active in the LMS and that match your predefined configuration settings for learning item types.

This feature currently supports integration with the SAP SuccessFactors Learning solution.

For more information, check SAP Help Portal and below Demo.


Video#3: DEMO of Integration with Learning Management System

Implementation Details

To set up the integration with SAP SuccessFactors Learning, you'll need the following:

  • A tenant on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) withSAP Integration Suite enabled.
  • An SAP SuccessFactors Learning system connected to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  • A business user with a business role in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system. The business role must contain the business catalogSAP_CORE_BC_COM (Communication Management), for example, the SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR (Administrator) business role.

For more information on the necessary setup and configuration activities, see Setting Up Integration with SAP SuccessFactors Learning.

Electronic Submission of Recordable Injury or Illness Cases to OSHA 

Business Background

OSHA stands for the Occupational Safety and Health Administration. It's a federal agency in the United States under the Department of Labor responsible for ensuring safe and healthful working conditions for workers by setting and enforcing standards and by providing training, outreach, education, and assistance.

This feature enables to submit injury and illness data to OSHA in the USA electronically and ensure compliance with OSHA's electronic reporting requirements.

Prerequisites

To submit injury and illness data to the OSHA electronically, the following prerequisites must be fulfilled:

  • You have completed the necessary setup and configuration activities. For more information, see the following sections:
    • Setting Up Electronic Submission to OSHA (USA)
    • Configuration for Regulation-Based Reporting
  • You have ensured that the following information is available in the incident record of each case that you want to report:
    • Injured person
    • Incident location 

Note:

The Establishment ID and Establishment Name identifiers must be assigned to the incident location in the Manage Locations app.

  • The OSHA (USA) regulation has been assigned to the incident.
  • On the Reports tab of the incident record, an OSHA 301 form has been added for each injured person, with the Reporting Required field set to Yes.

Key Feature

To report work-related injury and illness data electronically, carry out the following steps:

  1. Create an electronic submission.
    In the Manage Electronic Submissions app (F8152), create a record and specify the location (establishment) for which you are reporting, the applicable regulation, and the relevant reporting period.
  2. Generate the required data.
    Generate the data required to be submitted electronically according to the selected regulation. The system extracts the relevant data and generates the following reports:
    OSHA 301 (Injury and Illness Incident Report)
    OSHA 300 (Log of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses)
    OSHA 300A (Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses)
  3. Send the data.
    Send the relevant data to OSHA for validation. The system displays the web service response detailing the status of each report. In case of errors, you can correct the data and resend it.
  4. Complete the electronic submission.
    When all required injury and illness data has been sent successfully, complete the electronic submission, which finalises the reporting process.

For more information, check Electronic Submission of Injury and Illness Data to OSHA (USA) and below Demo.


Video#4: Demo Electronic Submission of Injury and Illness Data to OSHA

Automatic Generation of Emissions Declarations

Business Background

As an Environmental Manager, you can schedule the periodic generation of emissions declarations within the Manage Compliance Scenario app, In this release, during the creation of Emissions Declaration Scenario, under the Basic Information tab, there's a new section called Emissions Declaration Settings available.

With this setting, you can set up the scenario so that the system automatically generates an emissions declaration based on the specified frequency.

Key Feature

When you open or create an emissions declaration scenario, under the Basic Information tab, there's a new section called Emissions Declaration Settings. This section allows you to set up the scenario so that the system automatically generates an emissions declaration once every chosen reporting period.

You can schedule an emissions declaration for a future reporting period, and you can also schedule one to be generated for a past reporting period with the start date set as early as the current date. Every change to the emissions declaration settings is reflected across all existing scenario revisions, and when you delete a compliance scenario, all the scheduling data is deleted along with it.

If you have pre-existing scenarios of the Emissions Declaration type in your system prior to the current release, this feature won't be automatically applied. To enable this function for such scenarios, you can select the Set Up Automatic Generation button to the right of the Emissions Declaration Settings section, and then the section will become available for editing.

Check Automatic Generation of Emissions Declarations and below Demo.


Video#5: Demo Automatic Generation of Emissions Declarations.

Implementation Details

Authorizations

To access the Manage Compliance Scenario app to schedule emissions declarations, your user must have the following business catalog assigned: SAP_BR_EHS_ENV_MGMT (Environmental Manager).

Configuration

In your configuration environment, you can manage the menu options available in the Emissions Declaration Settings section using the following configuration activities:

  • Specify Reporting Period Types
  • Specify Recurrence Types for the Environmental Scheduler
  • Specify Contexts for the Environmental Scheduler

The following BAdIs are also available for developer extensibility:

  • Specify Recurrence Types for the Environmental Scheduler
  • Specify Contexts for the Environmental Scheduler
  • Schedule Jobs for Automatic Execution
  • Specify Reporting Period Types

For more information on configuration, see Configuration for Environment Management.

Create and Manage Requests for Chemical Approval

Business Background

In today's dynamic and complex industrial landscape, companies are constantly innovating and expanding their operations to stay competitive. This often involves the introduction of new chemicals into manufacturing processes, laboratory experiments, and other industrial applications. While innovation is essential for growth, it also poses significant risks to worker safety and the environment.

Companies across various sectors, from manufacturing and pharmaceuticals to agriculture and research, frequently deal with a wide array of chemicals. These chemicals can range from benign substances to highly hazardous materials. The proper management of these chemicals is not just a matter of compliance with regulations, but also a critical component of sustainable and responsible business operations.

Key Feature


Figure#1: Create and Manage Requests for Chemical Approval

With the new release, you can create requests for the approval of a new chemical in your company as well as display and continue to work on existing requests as shown in above figure#1.

You can ensure that all safety and environmental considerations associated with chemical usage are thoroughly evaluated. This feature also helps prevent incidents by conducting expert safety assessments and involving the appropriate responsible personnel before any chemical is utilized within your organization.

This feature provides a work view which contains the compliance relevant information that is available for a given raw material or unpackaged product. This information is needed as part of a request for chemical approval. The work view is sectioned and grouped identically to chapters 1 - 9 of the safety data sheet.

For more information, please check Create and Manage Requests for Chemical Approval.

Quick Links

SAP Help Portal:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/323bd3f1dc8248bc8647c62f1baa6a3a/3e0035755e414da09e2b57804d914b9d.html

What’s new:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/350270373b444800872c3bd19b4314c0.html

Where to Find More Information

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

For more information about Environment, Health, and Safety, please see the Link Collection Blog.

Follow us on @SAP and #S4HANA, and myself on LinkedIn .

📽️ Watch the Replays of Our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 Early Release Series!

From Jan 21 to Jan 24, we hosted a series of 21 compelling live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find a 50-minute-long recording and a presentation for Environment, Health, and Safety as well as 19 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime.
